metadata.filed_by: Mr. Rajesh Kumar, Security Guard, Gurgaon Central Mall

content: I, Rajesh Kumar, Security Guard, Gurgaon Central Mall, hereby file this First Information Report regarding a hit-and-run incident that occurred on 17.03.2024 at approximately 18:58 hours near the pedestrian crossing adjacent to the mall’s main entrance. At around 18:55 hours, I was on patrol near the pedestrian zone when I heard a loud thud followed by screeching tires. I rushed to the scene and found the body of an elderly male, approximately 55-60 years old, lying on the zebra crossing with severe trauma to the head and limbs. There was no vehicle present. Nearby, I observed a shattered left headlight casing and a fragment of blue paint on the road. I immediately alerted the mall’s CCTV control room and called emergency services. The victim was identified later as Mr. Arjun Mehta, 58, resident of Sector 44. Based on CCTV footage from the east gate, a blue Mahindra XUV700 with partially obscured license plate was seen speeding away from the scene. No one stopped to assist. I request immediate investigation under Section 304A IPC and Section 134 of the Motor Vehicles Act. I have attached the CCTV timestamped footage and my signed statement.

I was waiting for the bus at the pedestrian crossing at around 6:55 PM when I saw a blue SUV coming from the direction of the mall. It was going very fast. I saw the driver — it was definitely a woman. She had a dark hoodie pulled over her head, and I could see her profile — long hair, slender neck. The car hit the man crossing the road and didn’t stop. I screamed, but she accelerated. I saw her turn right onto Sector 46 Road. I am 100% sure it was a woman. I have poor eyesight without glasses, but I was wearing them that evening. I remember the car clearly because I saw the same model near my daughter’s school last month.

I was at the bus stop near the mall at 7:00 PM on March 17. I had just left my office and was waiting for the 7:10 bus to Delhi. I heard a crash, but I didn’t see the vehicle. I only heard a woman shout — 'Oh God, what have you done?' — right after the impact. I turned around but saw no one. I didn’t see the driver. I didn’t know Arjun was crossing. I had a fight with him earlier that day about the audit report. He threatened to expose me. But I didn’t harm him. I took the 7:15 bus. I have my ticket and the station CCTV to prove it.

I was photographing sunset shots on Sohna Road from 6:30 PM to 9:00 PM on March 17. I was capturing the light on the hills near the golf course. I have over 40 geotagged photos on my phone from that time. I returned home at 9:15 PM. I had no idea about the accident until the police came to my garage the next day. My wife’s car — the blue XUV700 — was parked in the garage. I didn’t drive it that day. I use my own black Honda City. The headlight was broken, but I thought it had been damaged two weeks ago during the monsoon pothole incident. I didn’t repair it because I was busy with assignments. I swear I didn’t go near Sector 45 that evening.

I was attending a virtual board meeting for my client, NexaPay, from 7:15 PM to 9:00 PM on March 17. My Zoom session was recorded. I was in my home office, wearing a formal blouse, with my laptop on the desk. I had a glass of water beside me. I never left the room. I didn’t know my husband had taken the car. I only found out when the police called me at 9:30 PM. I had no idea the headlight was damaged. I had my car serviced on March 10, and it was fine. I filed an insurance claim on March 19 because I noticed the damage then — I thought it was from a minor scrape. I had no motive to harm Mr. Mehta. I barely knew him.

My brother Arjun was under immense stress in the last two weeks. He told me he had evidence of financial fraud in Vikas Mehta’s startup — money siphoned from a client trust account managed by Priya Sharma’s law firm. He said he was going to the police on March 18. He was scared. He didn’t tell me what the evidence was, but he showed me a folder labeled 'NexaPay Audit.' He was going to meet Vikas that evening to confront him. I didn’t know he was walking home. I found his phone at the scene. It was still on, last call was to me at 6:52 PM. I believe he was killed because he was going to expose them.

metadata.source: Gurgaon Central Mall - East Gate Camera
metadata.analyst: DSP Arun Mehta, Cyber Cell, Gurgaon

content: CCTV footage from East Gate Camera (ID: GC-MALL-E07) captured a blue Mahindra XUV700 (plate partially obscured by mud) approaching the pedestrian crossing at 18:54:12. The vehicle accelerated through the crossing at 18:54:56, striking a pedestrian. The driver, identifiable as a male with a dark hoodie, was alone in the vehicle. No female passenger visible. The vehicle turned right onto Sector 46 Road at 18:55:03. A second camera (GC-MALL-W03) at the west entrance captured the same vehicle passing at 18:55:18. No other blue SUVs were recorded in the vicinity between 18:45 and 19:05. The license plate is partially visible as 'HR-14-AB-7891' — registered to Priya Sharma. No vehicle matching this description was recorded entering or exiting the mall parking between 18:30 and 19:10.

1. Blue paint fragment recovered from pedestrian crossing (E002) was analyzed via FTIR and XRF spectroscopy. Match found with factory color code 'Azure Blue (Code: MB-2023)' used on Mahindra XUV700 models manufactured between 2021-2023. 2. Registered vehicle (HR-14-AB-7891) was repainted in 'Midnight Sapphire' (Code: MB-2024) on January 15, 2024, per service records. The factory color is obsolete and no longer used in production. 3. Shattered headlight casing (E003) matches the left headlight assembly of the registered vehicle, but the lens material is from a 2020 model, not the 2023 model registered. 4. Tire tread pattern (12.5 cm width) matches Michelin Latitude Tour HP tires — standard fitment on XUV700. 5. Conclusion: The paint fragment does not originate from the currently registered vehicle. The headlight casing is from a replacement part, inconsistent with the vehicle’s original equipment. The vehicle involved was likely modified or had non-original parts installed.

1. Photo gallery on device contains 42 images labeled 'Sohna Road Sunset Shoot - 17.03.2024.' Geotags show locations in Sohna Road between 18:00–19:30. However, metadata timestamps were altered. Original EXIF timestamps show the earliest image was taken on March 10, 2024, at 17:45. 2. Bluetooth logs show pairing with device 'Vikas_Mehta_iPhone' at 18:50:12 and 18:52:47. Location data from Bluetooth pairing corresponds to Sector 45, within 300m of pedestrian crossing. 3. Device location history (GPS) shows movement from home (Sector 44) to Sector 45 between 18:40–19:00, then return home at 19:05. 4. WhatsApp messages with Vikas Mehta were deleted between 18:56 and 19:00. Recovered fragments indicate: 'He’s gone. Clean the car. I’m on the bus.' 5. Conclusion: The photo gallery was fabricated to establish alibi. Device was physically present at the crime scene at time of impact.

Vehicle: Blue Mahindra XUV700, HR-14-AB-7891. Inspection conducted on March 18, 2024. Findings: 1. Front left fender has recent dent and paint mismatch. Paint chip analysis indicates repair occurred within 48 hours. 2. Left headlight replaced with used unit (VIN: XUV700-HL-2020-08). 3. Damage to fender consistent with high-velocity impact. 4. Garage owner’s invoice dated March 16, 2024, at 23:47, for 'Headlight Replacement & Fender Repair' — payment via UPI. 5. Report states damage 'at least 14 days old' — this is incorrect. The repair was completed on March 16. The report was amended at the request of the owner. Original draft report attached as Annexure A.

Deceased: Mr. Arjun Mehta, 58, male. Cause of Death: Multiple blunt force traumas consistent with high-velocity vehicular impact. Primary injuries: Fractured skull, bilateral rib fractures, pelvic fracture, internal hemorrhage. No defensive wounds. No alcohol or drugs detected in blood. Time of Death: Estimated between 18:50 and 19:00. No signs of prior assault. Clothing: Slight scuff marks on right shoe, consistent with tire tread. No other injuries. Conclusion: Death resulted from being struck by a motor vehicle while crossing a pedestrian crossing. No evidence of pre-existing medical condition contributing to death.

Audit of client trust account #CTA-7891 (NexaPay Client Fund) reveals irregular transfers totaling ₹42.7 lakhs between January 15, 2024, and March 10, 2024. Funds were transferred to personal accounts linked to Vikas Mehta via shell companies. Priya Sharma, as the managing partner, had signing authority. No formal approval from client board. Withdrawals coincided with Vikas’s startup’s cash crunch. On March 16, 2024, a final transfer of ₹8.5 lakhs was made. Arjun Mehta, as internal auditor for the client, had flagged these transfers in a draft report dated March 15, 2024. The report was never submitted due to 'administrative delay.' However, a draft was found on his encrypted cloud drive, with a note: 'If I disappear, send this to CBI.'

Investigation Summary: The incident occurred on March 17, 2024, at Sector 45 pedestrian crossing. The vehicle involved is registered to Priya Sharma, but evidence indicates her husband, Rohan Sharma, was the driver. Digital forensics confirm Rohan’s presence at the scene via Bluetooth and GPS. His alibi via geotagged photos was fabricated. The mechanical inspection report was falsified by the garage owner, who admitted to altering the damage age upon request. The paint fragment does not match the currently registered vehicle, suggesting the vehicle had been modified with an older headlight and paint. Witness Anjali Kapoor’s testimony of a female driver is inconsistent with CCTV and appears to be a misidentification due to poor eyesight. Vikas Mehta’s alibi is partially corroborated by bus ticket and station CCTV, but his phone was paired with Rohan’s at the time of impact. Financial records confirm Arjun Mehta was about to expose a fraud involving Vikas and Priya’s law firm. Motive: To silence Arjun Mehta. Suspects: Rohan Sharma (driver), Vikas Mehta (conspirator). Priya Sharma’s alibi via Zoom is compromised — her screen recording shows a 4-minute gap (18:58–19:02) where she was not visible. Her insurance claim filed two days after the incident suggests awareness of damage. Case is recommended for charge sheet under Sections 304A, 201, 120B IPC and MV Act violations. Recommend arrest of Rohan Sharma and Vikas Mehta. Priya Sharma to be questioned further.

IN THE COURT OF THE SESSIONS JUDGE, GURGAON

CHARGE SHEET UNDER SECTION 173 CrPC

Accused:
1. Rohan Sharma, S/o Harish Sharma, aged 34, resident of Sector 44, Gurgaon
2. Vikas Mehta, S/o Baldev Mehta, aged 31, resident of Sector 50, Gurgaon

Offenses:
1. Section 304A IPC - Causing death by negligence
2. Section 201 IPC - Causing disappearance of evidence
3. Section 120B IPC - Criminal conspiracy
4. Section 134 of Motor Vehicles Act - Hit and Run

Facts:
On March 17, 2024, at approximately 18:56 PM, the accused Rohan Sharma, while driving a blue Mahindra XUV700 (HR-14-AB-7891), deliberately struck Mr. Arjun Mehta at a pedestrian crossing in Sector 45, Gurgaon, and fled the scene. The vehicle was owned by Priya Sharma, but Rohan was the driver. The act was committed in conspiracy with Vikas Mehta, who had a financial grudge against the deceased due to impending exposure of a ₹42.7 lakh fraud involving client trust funds managed by Priya Sharma’s law firm. Rohan Sharma fabricated an alibi using doctored photographs and tampered with vehicle evidence. Vikas Mehta was picked up by Rohan shortly before the incident and dropped off after. The mechanical inspection report was falsified. Digital evidence confirms the presence of both accused at the scene. The accused are jointly liable for the crime. Evidence includes CCTV footage, Bluetooth logs, digital forensics, autopsy report, and financial audit. The accused are charged and the case is ready for trial.
